name: rcnn

members.name: RCNNConfig
members.signature: RCNNConfig(num_classes: int = 80, in_channels: int = 3, roi_size: int = 227, dropout: float = 0.5, score_thresh: float = 0.05, nms_thresh: float = 0.5, max_detections: int = 300)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 80, in_channels: int = 3, roi_size: int = 227, dropout: float = 0.5, score_thresh: float = 0.05, nms_thresh: float = 0.5, max_detections: int = 300)

members.extended:
R-CNN (Girshick et al., CVPR 2014) applies a CNN independently to each
region proposal (warped to a fixed `roi_size × roi_size` crop), then
feeds the flattened pool5 features through two FC layers before the
classification and bounding-box regression heads.

Original paper uses AlexNet (5 conv + 3 FC) as the backbone.  The
effective output of pool5 for a 227 × 227 input is 6 × 6 × 256 = 9 216.

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
Number of foreground object classes.
             Background is automatically added as class 0,
             giving (num_classes + 1) output logits.

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of input image channels (3 for RGB).

parameters.name: roi_size
parameters.description:
Each region proposal is warped to this square size
             before being forwarded through the backbone.
             Original paper: 227.

parameters.name: dropout
parameters.description: Dropout probability applied after fc6 and fc7.

parameters.name: score_thresh
parameters.description:
Minimum class-score threshold applied during
             post-processing (after softmax).  Boxes whose
             max class score is below this value are discarded.

parameters.name: nms_thresh
parameters.description: IoU threshold for per-class NMS at inference time.

parameters.name: max_detections
parameters.description:
Maximum number of detections returned per image
             after NMS.

members.name: RCNNForObjectDetection

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, proposals: list[Tensor] | None = None)
methods.summary: Run R-CNN on a batch of images.

parameters.name: proposals
parameters.description:
list of B tensors, each (N_i, 4) xyxy region proposals.
       Pass `None` to return empty detections.

returns.description:
`ObjectDetectionOutput` with:
`logits`     : (Σ N_i, num_classes + 1) raw class logits.
`pred_boxes` : (Σ N_i, 4) decoded xyxy boxes (top class).

methods.name: postprocess
methods.signature: postprocess(output: ObjectDetectionOutput, proposals: list[Tensor] | None = None)
methods.summary: Apply score threshold + per-class NMS to raw R-CNN output.

parameters.name: proposals
parameters.description:
Same proposal list passed to `forward()`.  When
       omitted, falls back to `output.proposals`.

returns.description:
List of per-image result dicts, each with:
`"boxes"`  : (K, 4) xyxy kept detections
`"scores"` : (K,)   class confidence scores
`"labels"` : (K,)   integer class indices (1-based, 0=bg)

members.summary: R-CNN object detector (Girshick et al., CVPR 2014).
members.extended:
The first deep-learning detector to demonstrate that ImageNet-pretrained
CNN features transfer to localisation.  At inference the pipeline runs an
externally-supplied region-proposal set (typically from selective search)
through an AlexNet-style convolutional trunk *per proposal*, then feeds
the flattened pool5 features into a two-layer FC trunk with sibling
classification (`cls_head`) and bounding-box regression (`bbox_head`)
outputs.  This module covers the CNN + heads — proposals must be supplied
by the caller; `postprocess()` applies score thresholding and per-class
NMS to recover final detections.

parameters.name: config

examples:
Run inference on a single image with three external proposals:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.rcnn import rcnn
>>> model = rcnn(num_classes=80)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 600, 600)
>>> proposals = [lucid.tensor(
...     [[10.0,  10.0, 200.0, 200.0],
...      [50.0,  60.0, 300.0, 280.0],
...      [100.0, 80.0, 450.0, 400.0]])]
>>> out = model(x, proposals)
>>> out.logits.shape, out.pred_boxes.shape
((3, 81), (3, 4))
Apply post-processing (score threshold + per-class NMS) on the raw
output to obtain final per-image detections:
>>> detections = model.postprocess(out, proposals)
>>> set(detections[0].keys()) == {"boxes", "scores", "labels"}
True
notes:
See Girshick et al., "Rich Feature Hierarchies for Accurate Object
Detection and Semantic Segmentation", CVPR 2014 ([arXiv:1311.2524](https://arxiv.org/abs/1311.2524)).
For each proposal $P$ with centre $(P_x, P_y)$ and size
$(P_w, P_h)$, the bounding-box regressor predicts a
parameterised offset $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ so that the
refined box centre and size are



$$
\begin{aligned}
    \hat{G}_x &= P_w t_x + P_x, & \hat{G}_y &= P_h t_y + P_y, \\
    \hat{G}_w &= P_w e^{t_w},   & \hat{G}_h &= P_h e^{t_h}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The original paper trains per-class linear SVMs on top of frozen CNN
features; this implementation follows the more common modern
softmax-end-to-end form (matching the Caffe reference code).  Cost
is dominated by the redundant per-proposal CNN forward — the main
motivation for the Fast R-CNN successor's shared feature map.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: conv_features
attributes.description:
AlexNet conv1-conv5 trunk applied to each warped RoI crop.  Output
is a 9216-dim vector for the default `roi_size=227`.

attributes.name: fc_head
attributes.description:
Two-layer FC trunk (`fc6`, `fc7`) followed by sibling
`lucid.nn.Linear` heads:
`cls_head` produces $(N, K+1)$ class logits (background +
`num_classes` foreground classes) and `bbox_head` produces
$(N, 4K)$ class-specific bounding-box deltas.

members.name: rcnn
members.summary: R-CNN with AlexNet backbone (Girshick et al., CVPR 2014).
members.extended:
Builds a `RCNNForObjectDetection` with the paper-cited AlexNet
topology: five-conv trunk applied to each region proposal warped to
$227 \times 227$, followed by two FC layers and sibling
classification (`num_classes + 1`) and class-specific bounding-box
regression (`4 \cdot num_classes`) heads.  Original PASCAL VOC 2010
test mAP of 53.3% (paper Table 9) — a ~20-point jump over the prior
DPM state of the art.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored
— the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `RCNNConfig` (e.g.
`num_classes=20` for PASCAL VOC, `score_thresh=0.3` for a
tighter inference threshold, `roi_size=224` to match a
non-default backbone receptive field).

returns.description:
Detector with the AlexNet R-CNN configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.rcnn import rcnn
>>> model = rcnn(num_classes=20)   # PASCAL VOC
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 600, 600)
>>> proposals = [lucid.tensor(
...     [[10.0, 10.0, 200.0, 200.0],
...      [50.0, 60.0, 300.0, 280.0]])]
>>> out = model(x, proposals)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 21)
notes:
See Girshick et al., "Rich Feature Hierarchies for Accurate Object
Detection and Semantic Segmentation", CVPR 2014 ([arXiv:1311.2524](https://arxiv.org/abs/1311.2524)).
Region proposals must be supplied externally to `forward` —
the original paper uses selective search (~2000 proposals per image),
but any class-agnostic proposal method works.  At inference, classes
are scored via softmax over the $K + 1$ logits and bounding
boxes are refined per top-class delta:



$$
\hat{G}_w = P_w \exp(t_w),\quad
\hat{G}_x = P_w t_x + P_x.
$$

summary: R-CNN — object detection (Girshick et al., CVPR 2014).
extended:
Paper: "Rich feature hierarchies for accurate object detection
        and semantic segmentation"
citation: Girshick, Ross, et al. "Rich Feature Hierarchies for Accurate Object Detection and Semantic Segmentation." Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2014, pp. 580–587.
theory:
R-CNN (Regions with CNN features) is the first deep-learning
detector to demonstrate that ImageNet-pretrained CNN features
transfer to localisation.  The pipeline is deliberately modular:

1. **Region proposals.**  An external method (selective search in
   the original paper) yields $\sim 2000$ class-agnostic
   candidate windows per image.
2. **CNN feature extraction.**  Each proposal is *warped* to a
   fixed $227 \times 227$ patch and forwarded through an
   ImageNet-pretrained CNN (AlexNet).  The `pool5` activations
   (effectively $6 \times 6 \times 256 = 9216$ features) are
   flattened and passed through two fully-connected layers.
3. **Per-class linear SVMs** score each region; a class-specific
   bounding-box regressor refines the proposal coordinates with
   a parameterised $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ offset.

The cost is dominated by **redundant CNN forward passes** — one
per proposal — because no feature sharing across overlapping
regions exists.  Despite that inefficiency, R-CNN jumped mean
average precision on PASCAL VOC 2012 from $\sim 35\%$
(DPM) to $53.3\%$, making CNNs the dominant detection
paradigm and motivating every successor in the family
(Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN, Mask R-CNN).
